name: WAL with Group Commit
overview: TrustDB uses a Write-Ahead Log with batched group commits for durability. Multiple concurrent writes are coalesced into a single fsync, achieving 8-10× throughput improvement over per-write fsync. The WAL uses HMAC-SHA384 chained integrity (FabricRS PR #247: each tag covers prev_tag + WalProvenance + data; tamper-detection per NIST AU-10) and follows a Copy-on-Write design rather than the traditional ARIES recovery protocol. NOTE 2026-05-13: prior text said 'FNV32 checksums' — that was pre-PR-#247 stale; HMAC-SHA384 is the canonical integrity primitive.

howItWorks.title: Write Buffering
howItWorks.description: Incoming writes are appended to an in-memory WAL buffer. Each write gets a monotonic Log Sequence Number (LSN). The buffer accumulates writes until a commit trigger fires.

howItWorks.title: Group Commit Trigger
howItWorks.description: A commit is triggered by either: (a) buffer size exceeding threshold, (b) a configurable time window expiring, or (c) an explicit sync request. All buffered writes are flushed as a single batch with one fsync.

howItWorks.title: HMAC-SHA384 Chained Integrity
howItWorks.description: Each WAL record gets format [0xCB magic][provenance:26B][record:N][hmac_tag:48B]. Per-record WalProvenance (agent_id, clearance, connectivity, timestamp). Each HMAC tag = HMAC(key, prev_tag || provenance || data) — tampering any record invalidates all subsequent. RDKC-derived or static key modes. ACVP test vectors (RFC 4231). Provides tamper-proof non-repudiation per NIST AU-10. FabricRS PR #247.

howItWorks.title: CoW Recovery
howItWorks.description: Unlike ARIES (which uses undo/redo logs), TrustDB's CoW design means old pages are never overwritten. Recovery simply discards uncommitted WAL entries and reconstructs the tree from the last committed root. No undo pass is needed.

comparisons.source: Mature implementation but more complex recovery
Undo+redo passes; more complex but handles more edge cases

comparisons.narrative: Group commit is a well-understood technique used by PostgreSQL, MySQL, and most mature databases. TrustDB's implementation is straightforward and effective. The key differentiator is the CoW recovery model, which simplifies recovery at the cost of not supporting in-place updates. PostgreSQL's ARIES-based recovery handles more edge cases but is significantly more complex.

competitors.approach: WAL with ARIES-style undo/redo recovery and group commit
competitors.limitation: More complex recovery; handles more edge cases; mature and battle-tested
Single writer limits concurrency; simpler but less parallel

competitors.approach: WAL with group commit and configurable sync
competitors.limitation: LSM-tree based; no CoW; different write amplification characteristics

competitors.approach: CoW B-tree with no WAL needed (single-writer CoW is inherently safe)
competitors.limitation: No WAL overhead at all, but single-writer only; TrustDB needs WAL for concurrent writers
